turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-900, Dewberry Group versus Dewberry Engineers.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This is a trademark infringement suit between two real estate companies, each with the word Dewberry in its name. Dewberry Engineers provides real estate development services in the southeast region of the United States. It owns a registered trademark in the word Dewberry. The trademark gives the company exclusive rights to use that name in offering real estate services. Dewberry Group is another real estate company operating in the same region. Because Dewberry Engineers holds a trademark in the word Dewberry, Dewberry Group is not supposed to use that word in marketing its own real estate services, but the Group ignored that prohibition. It knowingly used the word Dewberry thereby infringing on the Engineers' trademark. So, as might be expected, the Engineers brought a trademark infringement suit against Dewberry Group and the Engineers won. The question this case asks is how much money Dewberry Engineers is entitled to recover. The federal trademark statute allows a prevailing party, like the Engineers to, this is the phrase, the defendant’s profits resulting from a trademark infringement. The named defendant in this suit was Dewberry Group, but here’s the rub. Dewberry Group had no profits at all on its books. The Group is one company in a network of separately incorporated affiliated companies all owned by a man named, John, you guessed it, Dewberry, and he set the companies up so that all the profits gained from the group’s infringing conduct went not to the group itself but instead to affiliated companies. The lower courts in this case thought that didn’t matter. They decided to treat as the defendant all of the Dewberry affiliates. In the Court’s words, to treat them all as a single entity, and once that was done, the defendant’s profits amounted to quite a lot, $43 million. Here Dewberry Group is challenging that award. We think Dewberry Group has a good point under the trademark statute forcing us to vacate the $43 million award. Remember that the statute allows payment of the defendant’s profits and the only named defendant in this case was Dewberry Group. The Engineers made a choice not to sue all of the group’s affiliates. And background principles of corporate law view affiliates like these as separate and independent except in rare circumstances. So the Engineers' suit against Dewberry Group did not automatically extend to its affiliates, and that means the courts were wrong to treat Dewberry Group and its affiliates as a single entity and to add all their profits together for purposes of making an award. The courts did not sufficiently respect corporate formalities, the principle of corporate separateness. In so holding though, we do not say the Dewberry Engineers cannot get any damages on remand, we leave a number of issues unanswered about whether and how courts can deal with a situation like this one where a defendant’s book profits do not seem to reflect the true financial gains it has received from an infringement. That is for the Court’s below to work out. We hold only that those courts cannot order as they did here the disgorgement of an affiliate’s profits as the defendant’s own. The opinion of the Court is unanimous. Justice Sotomayor has filed a concurring opinion.
